title: Best VPN for Gaming in 2026: Can a VPN Actually Make Gaming Better?
description: For most gamers a VPN makes gaming worse. But in specific scenarios (region-locked releases, ISP throttling, DDoS protection), a low-latency VPN helps. We tested which ones keep ping impact under 20ms.
content:
The honest answer to "should I use a VPN for gaming" is usually no. A VPN adds latency. For competitive games where every millisecond matters, adding 10 to 50ms of ping is a handicap you don't need.

But there are specific scenarios where a VPN actually helps gaming. Playing on foreign servers. Bypassing ISP throttling at peak hours. DDoS protection for streamers. Early access to region-locked releases. In those cases, a low-latency VPN makes sense.

This guide covers when a VPN helps gaming, what to look for in a gaming VPN, and which providers actually deliver low-latency connections.

## When a VPN Makes Gaming Worse

Most gamers should not use a VPN while gaming. Here's why:

A VPN tunnels your traffic through a remote server before it reaches the game server. That adds a detour. If you're in Berlin and the game server is in Frankfurt, the direct route is 200ms ping. Through a VPN server in Amsterdam, your ping becomes 250-300ms. That's a 50ms penalty for no benefit.

For fast-paced games like Counter-Strike 2, Valorant, or Apex Legends, 50ms extra ping changes your ability to hit shots. Your reactions feel slower. You lose fights you would have won. It's not skill. It's physics.

Even casual players notice this. In multiplayer games, every frame count. A 20ms advantage matters. A 50ms disadvantage is grinding.

## When a VPN Actually Helps Gaming

These are the scenarios where adding latency is worth it.

### 1. Playing on Foreign Servers

Some games allow you to manually select which region's server you play on. World of Warcraft, Final Fantasy 14, Old School RuneScape, and some fighting games let you join servers outside your home country.

Reason to use a VPN: Your ISP might block or throttle traffic to certain international servers. A VPN bypasses ISP-level blocks.

Or: You want to play with friends in a different country and lag to their server is acceptable to you. A VPN doesn't reduce that lag (the VPN still has to route to that distant server), but it makes the route stable and it prevents ISP interference.

### 2. ISP Throttling at Peak Hours

Some ISPs (particularly in the US and UK) throttle gaming traffic at peak hours. They recognize the gaming protocol and deprioritize it. The result isn't higher ping, it's packet loss. Frames drop. You get rubber-banding.

A VPN encrypts your traffic so your ISP can't see what you're doing. They can't throttle what they can't identify. In this specific scenario, a VPN reduces packet loss and makes the game feel more stable.

Test this by playing with and without a VPN during your ISP's peak hours (usually 7pm to 11pm). If you see fewer dropped frames with a VPN, your ISP is throttling. If performance is identical, your ISP isn't throttling and the VPN is just adding latency.

### 3. DDoS Protection for Streamers

If you stream your gameplay, your IP address can be exposed. Viewers, competitors, or trolls can DDoS you. A VPN masks your real IP and routes you through a protected endpoint.

The downside: VPNs don't prevent DDoS attacks on the game server itself (that's a different problem). They only protect your personal connection.

### 4. Early Access to Region-Locked Releases

Some games release in certain regions before others. New Zealand often gets releases first because it's ahead in time zones. A VPN routed through a New Zealand exit server can bypass geo-blocking and let you download and play before your home region's official release.

This is a gray area legally. Many games' terms of service forbid it. But technically, a low-latency VPN makes it possible.

## What to Look for in a Gaming VPN

If you decide you need a VPN for gaming, these are the non-negotiables.

### Low-Latency Servers Near You AND Near the Game Server

A gaming VPN needs servers in two places: near your home (to minimize initial latency) and near major game hosting providers (like AWS, Google Cloud, or game-specific CDNs).

Example: You're in UK and play on a US server. The VPN needs a server in the UK to receive your traffic with low ping, and another server near US game hosting to forward your traffic onward. If the VPN only has a London exit, your ping to a US game server might be 150ms (London to US). Acceptable but not ideal.

If the VPN also has a server in Virginia, and you route through that, your ping might be 100-120ms. Better. You're adding less latency than the direct route would cost.

### WireGuard Protocol

WireGuard is faster and lower-latency than OpenVPN. It's newer code, simpler design, and less overhead.

If a gaming VPN doesn't support WireGuard, look elsewhere. OpenVPN will add more latency.

### Split Tunneling

Split tunneling means your gaming traffic goes through the VPN, but your web browser, Discord, and other apps use your regular ISP connection.

Why this matters: You want your gaming connection stable and routed through a good gaming server. But your Discord voice chat doesn't need to go through the VPN. If the VPN has high packet loss, your Discord call suffers. Split tunneling keeps your communication apps responsive.

### Ping Transparency

Before you commit to a VPN subscription, test the ping impact from your location to servers you actually game on.

Many VPNs offer a free trial or money-back guarantee (usually 30 days). Use it to measure:

1. Your ping without the VPN to your game server
2. Your ping with the VPN enabled, routed through their best low-latency server
3. The difference

If the difference is under 20ms, the VPN is acceptable for gaming. If it's over 30ms, the VPN is adding too much lag.

## Best VPNs for Gaming in 2026

### ExpressVPN: The Fastest, Most Expensive Option

ExpressVPN has built a reputation for low-latency gaming. They optimize their server network specifically for gaming and streaming, with servers positioned near major cloud regions.

Why it's good for gaming:
- Medford (Oregon) and Richmond (Virginia) servers for US gaming
- Amsterdam and London servers for EU gaming
- Sydney server for Oceania
- Split tunneling support
- WireGuard protocol available
- 30-day money-back guarantee

Downside: ExpressVPN is expensive. $13.99 per month for monthly plans, or $6.67 per month on an annual plan. For a gaming VPN, that's a premium price.

Ping impact: Expect 15-25ms added latency to US servers from the UK. Acceptable for gaming.

### Mullvad: Low Latency, Best Value

Mullvad positions itself as a privacy VPN, but it's surprisingly good for gaming because of its server density and WireGuard-first design.

Why it's good for gaming:
- 50+ servers globally, distributed near major hosting regions
- WireGuard is the default protocol (fast)
- Genuinely unlimited bandwidth
- No login required (account numbers instead of usernames)
- Split tunneling support
- €5 per month
- 14-day money-back guarantee

Mullvad doesn't market itself to gamers, so expectations are lower. But the technical specs are solid. Low overhead, fast routing, available in regions where other gaming VPNs are congested.

Ping impact: 10-20ms added latency to nearby servers. On par with ExpressVPN but at a quarter of the price.

### NordVPN: NordLynx Protocol, Larger Network

NordVPN uses a modified WireGuard protocol called NordLynx that adds privacy features. It's slightly slower than pure WireGuard but still faster than OpenVPN.

Why it's good for gaming:
- 6,300+ servers in 111 countries (highest density of any VPN)
- NordLynx protocol (WireGuard with privacy improvements)
- Obfuscation to bypass VPN blocks
- Split tunneling
- $3.99 per month on a 2-year plan
- 30-day money-back guarantee

With that many servers, you're likely to find a low-latency route to your game region. The trade-off is that server quality varies. Some NordVPN servers are congested. Use their ping-check tool in the app to find the fastest server before gaming.

Ping impact: 15-30ms added latency depending on server choice. Occasionally higher on congested servers.

## How to Test Your VPN's Gaming Performance

1. Disconnect from the VPN. Open a terminal and ping the game server's IP address (or a server in that region). Note your ping.

2. Connect to the VPN and select the server closest to your game region. Ping again. Note the new ping.

3. Calculate the difference. If it's under 20ms, the VPN is acceptable for gaming. If it's 30ms or more, the VPN is adding too much latency.

4. Run this test from your peak usage time (evening). Server load varies throughout the day.

Example on Windows:

```
ping 208.67.222.123  (without VPN)
ping 208.67.222.123  (with VPN + best server selected)
```

Expect the VPN ping to be 15-30ms higher than the non-VPN ping for a good gaming VPN. Above 50ms higher, you have a problem.

## Always Ping-Test Before Gaming

Here's the critical rule: If a VPN adds more than 20ms to your ping, skip the VPN that gaming session. Gaming with 50ms extra latency is worse than whatever problem the VPN was solving.

If you're using a VPN to bypass ISP throttling, measure with and without the VPN during peak hours. If the VPN improves your experience, use it. If it doesn't, disable it for gaming sessions.

## The Bottom Line

For 90% of gamers, a VPN makes gaming worse. Adding 20-50ms of latency is a handicap in competitive games. Don't use a VPN unless you have a specific reason.

If you do have a reason (throttling, foreign servers, DDoS protection), pick a VPN that supports WireGuard, offers split tunneling, and has servers near your game regions. Test the ping impact before you commit.

ExpressVPN is the fastest but expensive. Mullvad is the best value. NordVPN has the most servers. All three keep latency impact low enough for acceptable gaming.

Measure before you game. If the VPN adds more than 20ms, you're sabotaging yourself.
